## Page 1
Clinical whole-genome sequencing from routine
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded specimens: pilot
study for the 100,000 Genomes Project
Pauline Robbe, MSc1, Niko Popitsch, PhD2, Samantha J.L. Knight, PhD, FRCPath2, Pavlos Antoniou, PhD1,
Jennifer Becq, PhD3,M i a oH e ,P h D3, Alexander Kanapin, PhD4, Anastasia Samsonova, PhD4,
Dimitrios V Vavoulis, PhD1, Mark T. Ross, BA (Hons), DPhil3, Zoya Kingsbury3, Maite Cabes, BSc5,
Sara D.C. Ramos, MSc5, Suzanne Page, MSc5, Helene Dreau, MSc5, Kate Ridout, PhD1,
Louise J Jones, MD, PhD6, Alice Tuff-Lacey, BSc hons6, Shirley Henderson, MSc, PhD5,
Joanne Mason, PhD, BSc6, Francesca M Buffa, PhD7, Clare Verrill, BM, FRCPath8,
David Maldonado-Perez, Mres, PhD9,I o a n n i sR o x a n i s ,M D ,P h D9, Elena Collantes, MD, PhD9,
Lisa Browning, MB BC, FRCPath9, Sunanda Dhar, MD, FRCPath9, Stephen Damato, MBBS, MA9,
Susan Davies, MBBS, FRCPath9, Mark Caulfield, FMedSci6,10, David R. Bentley, DPhil3, Jenny C. Taylor, PhD2,11,
Clare Turnbull, MD, PhD6,9,12 and Anna Schuh, MD, PhD5,11,13; on behalf of the 100,000 Genomes Project
Purpose: Fresh-frozen (FF) tissue is the optimal source of DNA
for whole-genome sequencing (WGS) of cancer patients. However,
it is not always available, limiting the widespread application of
WGS in clinical practice. We explored the viability of using
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissues, available routi-
nely for cancer patients, as a source of DNA for clinical WGS.
Methods: We conducted a prospective study using DNAs from
matched FF, FFPE, and peripheral blood germ-line specimens
collected from 52 cancer patients (156 samples) following routine
diagnostic protocols. We compared somatic variants detected in
FFPE and matching FF samples.

ospective study using DNAs from
matched FF, FFPE, and peripheral blood germ-line specimens
collected from 52 cancer patients (156 samples) following routine
diagnostic protocols. We compared somatic variants detected in
FFPE and matching FF samples.
Results: We found the single-nucleotide variant agreement reached
71% across the genome and somatic copy-number alterations
(CNAs) detection from FFPE samples was suboptimal (0.44 median
correlation with FF) due to nonuniform coverage. CNA detection
was improved significantly with lower reverse crosslinking tempera-
ture in FFPE DNA extraction (80 °C or 65 °C depending on the
methods). Our final data showed somatic variant detection from
FFPE for clinical decision making is possible. We detected 98% of
clinically actionable variants (including 30/31 CNAs).
Conclusion: We present the first prospective WGS study of cancer
patients using FFPE specimens collected in a routine clinical
environment proving WGS can be applied in the clinic.
Genet Med advance online publication 1 February 2018
Key Words: clinical variant reporting; copy-number alteration;
formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE); somatic variants; whole-
genome sequencing
INTRODUCTION
With the progress in analytical capability and cost reduction,
it is widely accepted that whole-genome sequencing (WGS)
presents advantages over targeted platforms. 1,2 WGS, a single
test, is particularly valuable for investigating all variant types,
including single-nucleotide variants (SNVs), small insertions/
deletions (indels), and structural variants such as copy-
number alterations (CNAs). Indeed, the conventional multi-
modality testing currently employed in routine diagnostics
can rapidly exhaust the low amount of material available from
tumor specimens.

ions (indels), and structural variants such as copy-
number alterations (CNAs). Indeed, the conventional multi-
modality testing currently employed in routine diagnostics
can rapidly exhaust the low amount of material available from
tumor specimens.
3,4 Although several targeted sequencing
methods also allow the detection of all classes of mutations,
WGS presents an additional advantage of unbiased
sequencing.5–7 The comprehensive nature of WGS also
removes the need to redesign and validate additional tests.
Previous studies have demonstrated the feasibility of WGS for
cancer patients in the clinic 8 but focused on using high-quality
nucleic acids extracted from fresh-frozen tissue (FF) specimens
collected within a research infrastructure.9 However, FF speci-
mens are not routinely collected because formalin-fixed,

## Page 2
paraffin-embedded (FFPE) material is the specimen of choice for
histopathological diagnosis.10 DNA extracted from FFPE speci-
mens presents degradation due to specimen processing11 such as
nucleic acid fragmentation, DNA crosslinks, abasic sites leading
to localized DNA denaturation and strand breaks, and
deamination leading to C> T mutation artifacts,
12 which impede
downstream sequencing analysis.
Several studies have compared sequencing data obtained
from FF and FFPE specimens and have shown that FFPE
samples can be interrogated using targeted sequencing,
including whole-exome approaches.
13–20 However, there is a
paucity of data evaluating WGS data. 17,21,22 Furthermore,
most of these studies did not use a matched normal sample as
germ-line (GL) control and therefore somatic variants
detection has not been investigated rigorously. Only two
studies performing whole-exome sequencing considered
somatic SNVs (using GL sample data) in exonic regions in

e a matched normal sample as
germ-line (GL) control and therefore somatic variants
detection has not been investigated rigorously. Only two
studies performing whole-exome sequencing considered
somatic SNVs (using GL sample data) in exonic regions in
four cancer samples.
18,19
Previous studies have disagreed as to whether CNA calls
between FF and FFPE were comparable 16,22 or poorer in FFPE
samples.13 In the first two studies, low-depth WGS or whole-
exome sequencing without matching GL samples were used
and in the third study whole-exome sequencing of one patient
(FF, FFPE, and matched GL samples) was reported.
Therefore, there is a lack of knowledge regarding somatic
mutations detection performance using WGS from FFPE
specimens (in particular structural variants and variants in
noncoding regions).
In the present study, we addressed key technical questions
relating to WGS analysis of DNA from FFPE-derived cancer.
We present the largest study to date evaluating WGS data sets
obtained from 156 genomes from 52 matched FF tumor,
FFPE tumor, and peripheral blood GL samples routinely
collected as part of the diagnostic process. We detail
comprehensively the differences observed between FF and
FFPE sequence data and propose a method to optimize the
quality of FFPE-derived WGS data that will allow the
acquisition of genome-wide data for all patients with cancer,
including those for whom only FFPE material is available.
MATERIALS AND METHODS
Sample collection and processing
One hundred eighty-four consecutive patients with cancer
undergoing surgical resection with curative intent were
recruited with written informed consent for research use at
the Oxford University Hospitals Foundation Trust ’s Geno-
mics Medicine Centre. Studies, conducted in accordance with

nts with cancer
undergoing surgical resection with curative intent were
recruited with written informed consent for research use at
the Oxford University Hospitals Foundation Trust ’s Geno-
mics Medicine Centre. Studies, conducted in accordance with
the Declaration of Helsinki, were approved by all relevant
institutional ethical committees and regulatory review bodies.
The tissue samples collected from adjacent region of tissue
blocks were prepared as both FF and FFPE samples following
the usual protocol in National Health Service diagnostic
laboratories. A peripheral blood sample (2 ml) was collected
for each patient ( Supplementary Methods online). Nucleic
acids extraction and quality control are detailed in the
Supplementary Methods .
Whole-genome sequencing
TruSeq DNA PCR-Free libraries were prepared from blood
and FF tissues using 1 μg of input DNA according to the
manufacturer’s instructions (Illumina, San Diego, CA). FFPE
specimens were prepared using the Illumina FFPE-extracted
genomic DNA sample preparation and TruSeq Nano DNA
Library Prep (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA), according to
the manufacturer ’s instructions ( Supplementary Methods ).
Sequencing was performed on a HiSeq2500 (Illumina, San
Diego, CA, USA) to an average depth of coverage of 70 × for
tumor samples and 30 × for GL samples. Alignment is
detailed in the Supplementary Methods .
Somatic SNV, indel, and CNA calling
Somatic SNV detection was performed with Mutect v1.1.4, 23
Shimmer v0.1.1, 24 and Strelka 2.0.14. 25 Indel detection was
performed using Shimmer and Strelka. The combination
method called variants considered “high confidence” as they
were identified by all variant callers according to VCF Intersect.
(Supplementary Methods). Variants were validated using the

ection was
performed using Shimmer and Strelka. The combination
method called variants considered “high confidence” as they
were identified by all variant callers according to VCF Intersect.
(Supplementary Methods). Variants were validated using the
AmpliSeq Cancer Hotspot Panel (Supplementary Methods).
Log
2R was calculated from tumor-normal pair data using
BIC-seq v1.2.1. 26 Somatic CNAs were called and manually
curated with Nexus Discovery Edition 7.5 (BioDiscovery, El
Segundo, CA, USA) ( Supplementary Methods ).
FFPE DNA extraction optimization
Several reverse crosslinking temperatures (90 °C, 80 °C, 65 °C,
and 56 °C), incubation times (1 and 3 h), and buffer addition
(saline sodium citrate) were studied in 33 samples from 5
patients ( Supplementary Table S1 ) using two DNA
extraction kits ( Supplementary Table S2 ). To ensure
homogeneity of the different sample conditions extracted,
all proteinase K digested aliquots from the same patients were
pooled, homogenized, and split before the reverse crosslinking
step ( Supplementary Methods ).
Clinical reporting
Clinical reports were generated for five renal cases from FF and
independently from matching FFPE data. SNVs and indels from
Strelka 2.0.14 (ref. 25) were annotated using VEP GRCh37
release 85 (ref. 27) and only variants in the COSMIC cancer gene
census
28 (referencing 600 genes), and genes of the renal cell
carcinoma and PI3K-Akt signaling KEGG pathways were kept
for further analysis.
29 CNA calls were manually curated using
Nexus Discovery Edition 7.5 and annotated against the different
gene list of interest (detailed for SNVs and indels). Alterations
were divided into tiers to determine clinically actionable ones
(druggable/predictive/prognostic and/or with diagnostic/classifi-
cation implications

d annotated against the different
gene list of interest (detailed for SNVs and indels). Alterations
were divided into tiers to determine clinically actionable ones
(druggable/predictive/prognostic and/or with diagnostic/classifi-
cation implications
30)( Supplementary Methods).
RESULTS
A significant number of samples lost to the study due to
poor quality

patient. Of 184 patients, 87 (48%) were excluded due to lack
of a suitable FF sample and 30 of the remaining patients were
excluded because of the poor quality of FFPE DNAs or
libraries. Another 15 patients were excluded at various other
quality control (QC) steps. Trio sets were available for 52
cancer patients (10 breast, 12 colorectal, 7 endometrial, 4
prostatic, 14 renal, and 5 thoracic tissues) ( Supplementary
Figure S1A,B , Supplementary Table S3 ).
FFPE DNA quality control revealed short and denatured
DNA
The quantity and quality of the 52 matching FF and FFPE
DNAs were studied ( Supplementary Table S4 , Wilcoxon
signed rank test). The total nucleic acid yield assessed using
Nanodrop (Thermo Fisher Scientific, MA), measuring all
nucleic acids, was lower for FFPE than FF DNAs, without
reaching statistical significance ( p = 0.0781). The same
metric measured by Qubit dsDNA Broad Range Assay kit
(Thermo Fisher Scientific), assessing only double-stranded
DNA, showed a significantly lower DNA quantity for FFPE
samples (p = 1.41e
− 06). These results supported the assertion
that FFPE samples yielded similar amounts of total nucleic

e Assay kit
(Thermo Fisher Scientific), assessing only double-stranded
DNA, showed a significantly lower DNA quantity for FFPE
samples (p = 1.41e
− 06). These results supported the assertion
that FFPE samples yielded similar amounts of total nucleic
acids, but lowered double-stranded DNA quantity due to
denaturation. The A260/A280 ratio, a marker of nucleic acid
purity, was similar between FFPE and FF samples with mean
values of 1.89 and 1.88, respectively. However, when subjected
to gel electrophoresis the FFPE DNAs revealed nucleic acid
smears in the range o0.5–40 kb, indicating DNA fragmenta-
tion, whereas the matching FF samples presented a distinctive
band > 40 kb ( Supplementary Figure S2A,B ).
FFPE DNA presented shorter fragments and sequencing
data revealed nonuniform coverage

The mean sequencing depth was 77 × (50 –100 × ) for FFPE
samples and 93 × (78 –122 × ) for FF samples and was variable
across chromosomes ( Supplementary Figure S4 ). FFPE
samples presented a lower proportion of regions reaching
the targeted depth of 70 × : 0.351 versus 0.782 for the FF
samples ( Figure 1a ). The uniformity of coverage, crucial for
optimal variant detection ( Supplementary Methods ), was
calculated taking the standard deviation of read coverage to
measure extreme high and low signals ( Figure 1b ). The
median standard deviation was between 1.1 × and 5.8 ×
higher in FFPE than matching FF ( p o 2.2e
− 16) meaning
sequencing coverage was less uniform in FFPE data.
Purity determined by visual assessment of pathologists and
determined from sequencing data showed a statistically
significant positive correlation ( Supplementary Figure S5 ,
Supplementary Table S3 ). For 24% of cases, the estimated
purity was > 40% tumor content by visual assessment and was
o40% by sequencing.
Different numbers of somatic SNVs and indels detected in
FF and FFPE samples and variants agreement dependent
upon the variant caller
Firstly, SNV and indel data sets of all 52 patients from FFPE
and FF data were compared using several variant callers
(Supplementary Table S6 ). Globally, somatic SNVs were
increased in FFPE ( Supplementary Figure S6A ).

dent
upon the variant caller
Firstly, SNV and indel data sets of all 52 patients from FFPE
and FF data were compared using several variant callers
(Supplementary Table S6 ). Globally, somatic SNVs were
increased in FFPE ( Supplementary Figure S6A ).
FF and FFPE data sets were compared to identify variants
detected either only in FF or only in FFPE sample data ( “FF
unique” and “FFPE unique ”) and variants detected in both
samples ( “FF–FFPE overlap ”)( Supplementary Material ). A
similar number of SNVs was discovered in the FF –FFPE
overlap for Mutect (2.08 million) ( Figure 2a ) and Strelka
(1.95 million), but different in proportions of agreement (58
vs. 19%) ( Supplementary Figure S6B ). The high number of
FFPE-unique SNVs detected by Strelka considerably reduced
the positive predictive value (0.21) while presenting the
highest sensitivity at 0.77 ( Supplementary Table S7 ).
Variants detected in different regions of the genome were also
studied (Supplementary Table S8). A high FF –FFPE agreement
was detected in reliable regions (Genome in a Bottle
31)w h e r e a st h e
agreement was lower in regions of low complexity (Figure 2b).
Regarding indels, Shimmer detected more in FF samples
(Supplementary Figure S6C ), whereas Strelka detected more
indels in FFPE ( Figure 2a ) leading to different FF –FFPE
overlap and conflicting sensitivity and positive predictive
value patterns ( Supplementary Figure S6D ,Supplementary
Table S7). However, the number of high-risk variants (indels,
start and stop codon losses and gains) was not increased in
FFPE samples compared to FF ( Supplementary Figure S7 ).
With respect to tissue types, thoracic presented more SNVs
than other tissues ( Supplementary Figure S8 ) and the highest
proportion of agreement between FF and FFPE with all

not increased in
FFPE samples compared to FF ( Supplementary Figure S7 ).
With respect to tissue types, thoracic presented more SNVs
than other tissues ( Supplementary Figure S8 ) and the highest
proportion of agreement between FF and FFPE with all
variant callers ( Supplementary Figure S9A,B ).
Finally, the proportion of C > T base substitutions (known
to be enriched in FFPE specimen sequencing data that
underwent deamination) was higher in the data set produced
by Strelka and only to a lesser degree in data sets from other
tools ( Supplementary Figure S10A –D). This increase in
C > T mutations observed in the Strelka data set was
correlated with an increase of intergenic variants
(Supplementary Figure S11 ).
Tumor heterogeneity and sampling heterogeneity
explained differences between FF and FFPE samples
Fifty-two percent of variants were identified in the FF –FFPE
overlap, 21% were in the FF-unique data set, and 27% were in
the FFPE-unique data set ( Supplementary Figure S12A ).
Somatic SNVs unique in FF or FFPE samples were
investigated. The FFPE-unique data set presented a median
proportion of C > T substitutions of 24.2% (15.8 –42.3), which
was lower than in the overlap data set (36% (17 –53.9)),
confirming FFPE-unique variants were not deamination artifacts
(Supplementary Figure S13A ). The mutational spectrum
analysis showed a higher C > A/G > T substitution rate in
FFPE-unique mutations ( Supplementary Figure S13B ). The
allelic fractions (AFs) of FF-un ique and FFPE-unique variants
highlighted the presence of low-level variants ( Supplementary
Figure S12B ). This data clearly suggested that intratumor
heterogeneity (variants with low a llelic fractions that are truly
different between FF and FFPE tissue specimens) or sampling

highlighted the presence of low-level variants ( Supplementary
Figure S12B ). This data clearly suggested that intratumor
heterogeneity (variants with low a llelic fractions that are truly
different between FF and FFPE tissue specimens) or sampling
heterogeneity (rare variants present in the tissue specimens but
missed by sequencing because theindividual molecules with that
variant were not sampled) were important explanations for the
differences observed in SNV calling.
To observe the effect of tumor and sampling heterogene-
ities, we filtered out SNVs detected at low AFs using several
thresholds ( Figure 2c ). The SNVs overlap between FF and
FFPE increased dramatically when mutations with low AFs
were excluded. The amplitude of this effect varied in different
tissue types. Thoracic sample data achieved a median SNV
FF–FFPE overlap of 75% when filtering out variants with AFs
lower than 0.02, making this tissue type the best-performing
one ( Supplementary Table S9 ). Other tissue types reached a
median FF –FFPE overlap of 75% only if variants with AFs
lower than 0.12 or 0.22 were removed, depending on tissues.
SNVs detected in different tissue types were explored
further ( Supplementary Figure S14A –C, Supplementary
Figure S15A,B , Supplementary Figure S16 ). The SNV
overlap between FF and FFPE samples was found to
correlate with several metrics ( Supplementary Figure
S17A–F), but particularly with the distribution of allelic
fractions of somatic SNVs: the higher the median allelic
fractions, the higher the SNV overlap between FF and FFPE
samples ( Supplementary Figure S17G , Supplementary
Figure S18 ). In our cohort, most thoracic samples
presented a high distribution of allelic fractions and
therefore a high SNV overlap between FF and FFPE

higher the SNV overlap between FF and FFPE
samples ( Supplementary Figure S17G , Supplementary
Figure S18 ). In our cohort, most thoracic samples
presented a high distribution of allelic fractions and
therefore a high SNV overlap between FF and FFPE
samples (4 of 5), although this was not confined to this
tissue type (3 other cases involved) ( Supplementary Figure
S17G) and did not correlate with assessed tumor purity.
Finally, to limit the effect of tumor heterogeneity and
sampling heterogeneity, variants underpowered in matching

FF or FFPE samples were filtered from the data set using a
binomial distribution model ( Supplementary Figure S19 ):
the depth set was > 70 × at the position of the variant for both
FF and FFPE samples and the allelic fractions > 0.067 in one
of the two samples. With this approach, the SNV agreement
between FF and FFPE samples increased from 52 to 63%
when screening the whole genome ( Supplementary Figure
S12C) and 71% when selecting variants in reliable regions
(Genome in a Bottle regions, representing 69% of the
genome) ( Figure 2d ).
Somatic mutations in cancer driver genes accurately
detected from FFPE data sets
When considering all SNVs and indels, 90% of cancer-
associated genes from the COSMIC cancer gene census 28 were
found more frequently mutated in the FFPE data set than FF
data set ( Supplementary Figure S20A ). However, when
considering the most clinically relevant types of mutation
defined as functional mutations (exonic: missense, stop,
frameshift, and in-frame indel mutations and splicing
variants) > 99% of genes were not more mutated in FFPE

re S20A ). However, when
considering the most clinically relevant types of mutation
defined as functional mutations (exonic: missense, stop,
frameshift, and in-frame indel mutations and splicing
variants) > 99% of genes were not more mutated in FFPE
than FF data sets ( Supplementary Figure S20B ).
In addition, 73 variants identified in 207 targets in 46 clinically
relevant genes were validated in FF and FFPE using the

Scientific), an alternative method to the Illumina technology
(Supplementary Table S10 ). When using the panel as gold
standard, the sensitivities of WGS using FF and FFPE were found
to be 0.86 and 0.82, respectively, and the positive predictive values
were both 1. Of the 10 variants missed in FF and 11 in FFPE by
WGS, 8 had an AF o0.1 in the panel data ( Supplementary
Table S11). After inspection of WGS data in the Integrative
Genomics Viewer,
32 ad e l e t i o ni nVHL was confirmed in FF and
FFPE data as well as one TP53 deletion in FFPE data.
Copy-number intensity signal was noisy in the FFPE data set
Copy-number alterations (CNAs) were also evaluated. Log 2
ratio (Log 2R), showing increase intensity for genome
amplifications and decrease intensity for deletions, and the
corresponding B-allele frequency plots, showing variation of
the median signal for loss of heterozygosity, for tumor-normal
pairs were compared for FF ( Figure 3a ) and matching FFPE
samples ( Figure 3b ) (FF-GL independently to FFPE-GL).
This example illustrated that the data points are highly noisy
in FFPE samples, characterized by a larger spread of the
Log

Optimization of FFPE DNA extraction improved alignment
metrics and detection of SNVs, indels, and CNAs
To improve coverage uniformity and therefore CNA detection
we improved DNA integrity of FFPE samples. From the FFPE
sample preparation and DNA extraction, 33 preanalytical
variables were reviewed and we focused on the DNA
extraction process. We hypothesized that modifying the de-
crosslinking step would yield less fragmented nucleic acids
enriched in double-stranded DNA molecules. Five illustrative
cases from the 52 trios analyzed previously were selected
(Supplementary Table S1 ). New FFPE cores were collected
depending availability to test two commercially available
DNA extraction kits, four de-crosslinking temperatures,

s. Five illustrative
cases from the 52 trios analyzed previously were selected
(Supplementary Table S1 ). New FFPE cores were collected
depending availability to test two commercially available
DNA extraction kits, four de-crosslinking temperatures,
two incubation times, and two salt concentrations
(Supplementary Table S2 ).
Comparisons of sequencing alignment metrics were made to
estimate the potential improv ement in FFPE samples. Overall
they were equivalent or improved in the experimental FFPE data
sets compared with the initial FFPE data sets. In particular, AT
dropout ( Supplementary Figure S22A ), global sequencing
coverage, and coverage unif ormity were improved in all
experimental FFPE compared with initial FFPE data sets
(Supplementary Figure S23A,B). Regarding SNVs and indels,
the overlap between FF and experimental FFPE data sets was
higher than in FF and initial FFPE data sets ( Supplementary
Figure S22B), except one condition for patient 004.
CNA detection improvement was assessed by visualization
(Figure 4a) and calculation of correlation coefficients of Log
2R
between FF and experimental FFPE samples ( Figure 4b ,
Supplementary Figure S24 ). Correlation coefficients were
greatly improved for all exper imental FFPE samples and were
above 0.85 for three of five patients (patients 004, 065, and 365).
These results confirmed that WGS data from poor-quality FFPE
samples can be improved to enable more accurate detection of
small and large mutations.
Clinical report from optimized FFPE samples comparable to
that from FF samples
Clinical whole-genome reports, including somatic SNVs,
indels, and CNAs, were generated for the five patients using
the optimized experimental FFPE data. These were compared
with an independent analysis of the FF data ( Supplementary

from FF samples
Clinical whole-genome reports, including somatic SNVs,
indels, and CNAs, were generated for the five patients using
the optimized experimental FFPE data. These were compared
with an independent analysis of the FF data ( Supplementary
Table S12 ). Forty alterations were classified as tier 1 and
clinically actionable (Materials and Methods, Supplementary
Methods), 64 as tier 2, and 36 as tier 3 across the five patients
(Table 1). Of these alterations, 98% tier 1, 86% tier 2, and 78%
tier 3 were reported in both FF and FFPE. Twelve variants
were detected only in the FFPE data; all were SNVs and indels
and 7 of 12 had an AF lower than 0.13. Six variants, of which
three were CNAs, were detected only in FF tissue data. The
remaining 51 CNAs were detected from both FFPE and FF,
confirming the improved ability to detect CNAs from
optimized FFPE samples.
DISCUSSION
We have carried out the largest study to date generating and
comparing clinical WGS of cancer specimens obtained from
FFPE material and matching FF samples, to answer the
crucial question of whether WGS can be reliably introduced
in the clinic.
Of the 184 cases recruited, a full trio (GL, FF, and FFPE)
was obtained for 52, which clearly demonstrated the
challenges of collecting FF tissues prospectively in routine
clinical diagnostics and the DNA QC failure rates for
nonoptimized FFPE tissues. Although FF samples clearly
remain the optimal source of tumor DNA for WGS, limited
availability is a barrier to the widespread application of WGS
in clinical practice. The success rate of library preparation
from FFPE DNA was much higher than previously reported
15
(80 vs. 29.5%), due to fast processing from surgery to DNA
extraction (6 months or less), and adapting the amount of

ation of WGS
in clinical practice. The success rate of library preparation
from FFPE DNA was much higher than previously reported
15
(80 vs. 29.5%), due to fast processing from surgery to DNA
extraction (6 months or less), and adapting the amount of
input DNA based on DNA QC data ( Δ Cq assay, see the
Supplementary Material ).
Sample purity assessment by computational method generally
calculated lower tumor purity than pathologist visual assessment
of the specimen. Therefore initial assessment of purity of the
tissue and rigorous purity inclusion criteria are crucial to ensure
a sufficient detection power of subclonal variants.
Coverage depth was nonunifo rm alignment metrics were
suboptimal for FFPE data, as described in multiple
studies.
15,19,22,33 However, our results were influenced by lower
stringency of fragment size selection and the polymerase chain
reaction step included in the FFPE library preparation method
(see Materials and Methods) to improve library yield.
Unlike most previous studies focusing on GL
variants,
14,16,17,19–22,33–36 we were able to compare the
detection of somatic variants between FF and FFPE data.
Our results demonstrated a high discrepancy between these
data sets dependent on the analytical tool set employed,
validating previous observations.
36,37 Consistent with pre-
vious reports, this was due to the presence of low-AF
variants
17 caused by intratumor heterogeneity and sampling
heterogeneity leading to wrongly calling false-negative and
false-positive variants in FFPE data (even though efforts were
made to minimize this effect by collecting FF and FFPE
samples from adjacent region of tissue blocks). At best, after
power calculation and filtering, the somatic SNVs agreement
reached 71% with 12% of variants detected only in FFPE and

efforts were
made to minimize this effect by collecting FF and FFPE
samples from adjacent region of tissue blocks). At best, after
power calculation and filtering, the somatic SNVs agreement
reached 71% with 12% of variants detected only in FFPE and
17% of variants missed in FFPE. Although this result would
be insufficient in a clinical setting most of these variants are
found in noncoding regions, which are not clinically relevant
to date. WGS coverage is limited due to cost and this
technique cannot be as sensitive as targeted approaches to
detect low subclonal variants (AF o0.1). Indeed, in our
tumor data sets, nine clinically relevant variants with AF o0.1
called by the AmpliSeq cancer panel were not detected in
either FF or FFPE tissue samples. In future years, the
reduction in costs will allow for higher depth and a further
increase in WGS sensitivity.

Our study also highlighted poor concordance between FF and
FFPE somatic variants in regions of sequence complexity and
regions with reduced read mappability. In Genome in a Bottle
regions (representing 69% of the genome) higher concordance
was observed demonstrating that WGS data generated from
FFPE-extracted DNA presents advantages compared with whole-
exome sequencing, restricted to coding regions.
Somatic CNA detection was investigated. A limitation of
our approach was to combine coverage data from tumor with
normal coverage data derived from blood DNA samples. It
resulted in a fluctuation of Log
2R values. To increase CNA
detection accuracy in FFPE samples, we optimized the de-
crosslinking step during DNA extraction. This improved

data from tumor with
normal coverage data derived from blood DNA samples. It
resulted in a fluctuation of Log
2R values. To increase CNA
detection accuracy in FFPE samples, we optimized the de-
crosslinking step during DNA extraction. This improved
coverage uniformity, particularly in AT-rich genomic regions,
and resulted in calling independently 51 of 54 CNVs that had
been identified in corresponding FF samples. However, visual
inspection and manual curation was necessary, which is
feasible in diagnostics where sequencing data is analyzed for a
single patient at the time but can be challenging for cohort
analyses. New statistical and bioinformatics tools that take
specific features of FFPE-derived cancer sequencing data into
account (such as shorter fragment lengths with nonuniform
coverage) and that call CNVs and other types of structural
variants with greater confidence are therefore required.
Overall our clinical reporting from FFPE was successful
with 98% of clinically actionable variants (tier 1) identified.
All samples underwent “real-life” routine processing for
diagnostics to preserve tumor architecture. However, there is
currently no standardization of routine diagnostic FFPE
processing and the preparation varies considerably depending
on tissue types and sizes and according to different
laboratories. These variations in the preparation lead to
different DNA alterations likely to require different types of
optimization, making the widespread application of FFPE-
derived DNA for WGS an even greater challenge.
In conclusion, this pilot study for the 100,000 Genomes
Project represents the first prospective WGS study of cancer
patients comprehensively comparing results from FF and
paired FFPE specimens collected in a routine clinical
environment.

challenge.
In conclusion, this pilot study for the 100,000 Genomes
Project represents the first prospective WGS study of cancer
patients comprehensively comparing results from FF and
paired FFPE specimens collected in a routine clinical
environment. Despite the significant shortcomings of FFPE-
derived WGS data, we demonstrate that optimization of the
DNA extraction process combined with careful bioinfor-
matics analysis and visual data inspection allows confident
SNV/indel and CNV calling of clinically relevant variants for
diagnostic purposes. Our results support the use of optimized
FFPE cancer samples as an alternative source of DNA for
WGS cancer diagnostics if FF specimens are not available.

chunk_id: BSL2_documents_Färkkilä_lab/BSL1 and 2/Action Plan in case of an emergency _Färkkilä_Vähärautio_Cell Culture.docx::chunk_0000
source_file: BSL2_documents_Färkkilä_lab/BSL1 and 2/Action Plan in case of an emergency _Färkkilä_Vähärautio_Cell Culture.docx
text:
Last updated 10.9.2020, Anastasiya Chernenko
Action plan in case of an emergency situation in cell culture room 
In case cell culture rooms B324a1 ja B324a2 located in Biomedicum Helsinki building need to be urgently evacuated, for example, because of the water damage, all liquid cell and tissue cultures located in this rooms are either discarded by adding 2% Virkon Disinfectant-solution in water or, circumstances allowing, frozen and moved to deep freezer storage. Solid cell culture waste is discarded according to the Waste handling instructions - situation allowing, brought to P-floor’s cold waste storage to wait for a pick-up, if that room is not flooded. Cell and tissue culture volumes used are quite small, so major spread to the environment is not possible and thus is not a threat. In addition, cells are unlikely to survive in the conditions different from optimal used in cell culture incubator (temperature, pH, sterility). 
In case of fire cell cultures do not cause risk, as they will be easily destroyed by the high temperatures, neither they are able to form aerosols.
If cell culture HEPA-filtered laminar or cell culture CO2-incubator gets broken, work is stopped and the cultures are either destroyed by adding 2% Virkon Disinfectant-solution in water or moved to the other laminar or incubator or frozen and moved to the deep freezer for storage.
In case cells containing solution splashes onto one’s eye-, mouth- or nose mucous membranes,
the contaminated area is rinsed with running tap water for at least 10 minutes (EYE SHOWER is located in room B314, Medical Genetics, Right after Sampsa Hautaniemi office, behind ONCOSYS grey door). After that contact Occupational Heathcare doctor (MehiläinenYmpyrätalo, tel. 010 414 00).

chunk_id: BSL2_documents_Färkkilä_lab/BSL1 and 2/Action Plan in case of an emergency _Färkkilä_Vähärautio_Cell Culture.docx::chunk_0001
source_file: BSL2_documents_Färkkilä_lab/BSL1 and 2/Action Plan in case of an emergency _Färkkilä_Vähärautio_Cell Culture.docx
text:
ning tap water for at least 10 minutes (EYE SHOWER is located in room B314, Medical Genetics, Right after Sampsa Hautaniemi office, behind ONCOSYS grey door). After that contact Occupational Heathcare doctor (MehiläinenYmpyrätalo, tel. 010 414 00). 
Gloves and lab coat (optionally also face mask and goggles) are worn when working with cell lines or processing patient-derived samples. In case cell culture or patient material splashes contaminate skin, disinfect the affected skin area with 70% ethanol and then thoroughly wash with soap under running tap water. 
Stub and cut wounds are prevented by using protective gear (gloves, lab coats) as well as correct and safe working technique and appropriate tools.
If cell culture solution or patient material contaminates wounded skin (for example, if spills penetrate the wet lab coat) or under the skin through the stab – wash the affected area with 70% ethanol and after it with running tap water and soap, dry the skin. After that contact Occupational Healthcare doctor (MehiläinenYmpyrätalo, tel. 010 414 00). 
If biological substances originating from cell culture or patient material contaminate the centrifuge or cell culture room surfaces, spills are washed away with 1% Virkon Disinfectant-solution in water and then with 70% ethanol. If spills contaminate clothes, the stains are disinfected with 70% ethanol and/or trashed together with solid cell culture waste. 
In case Virkon solution and its powder meant for disinfection of the liquid wastes and spills unexpectedly finishes it is borrowed from the neighboring laboratory and then ordered via City-lab laboratory supplies company.

text:
solid cell culture waste. 
In case Virkon solution and its powder meant for disinfection of the liquid wastes and spills unexpectedly finishes it is borrowed from the neighboring laboratory and then ordered via City-lab laboratory supplies company.
In case -80oC and -150oC (B320 and B304b, correspondingly) deep freezers where cell lines and patient-derived samples are stored are malfunctioning all the stored materials are moved to the neighboring lab freezers or to 5th floor (-80oC ) and P-floor (-150oC) spare freezers. Deep freezers are connected to the centralized alarm system. In case of an alarm outside of the office hours the responsible persons (names and phone numbers are listed on the freezers’ doors as well as below) are contacted by a service worker in charge who first receives the alarm. The responsible person comes to the laboratory and moves the materials from the broken freezer to a spare freezer where they will stay until the malfunctioning equipment is fixed.
First aid cabinet, which includes among others bandages to treat small wounds, is located on the hallway wall next to the room B317a.
Occupational Healthcare contact details are: MehiläinenYmpyrätalo, tel. 010 414 00. 
Introductory safety training responsible persons are laboratory managers of each laboratory working in the cell culture room:

14.09.2020, Anastasiya Chernenko
Biosafety Manual for cell culture rooms B324a1, a2 (BSL 1)
Please read this manual carefully before starting any experiments. These rules apply to biosafety level 1 biological agents in rooms B324a1, a2. However, no set of rules can substitute for careful working procedures, use of common sense and consideration of others.
General rules
Biosafety level 1 (BSL1) organisms used in this room are cell lines and cultured patient tissues. Their use will be contained in the laminars and incubators marked with Biohazard-sign. You will not be allowed to work in this room unless you have received adequate training in safe cell culture techniques.
The cell culture door is kept closed at all times. Sticky mat is put on the floor at the room entrance to reduce the amount of contaminants introduced into the room by researchers. 
No eating or drinking is allowed in the laboratory. (Not even chewing gum!)
Before and after the work all the surfaces in use and researcher’s gloves must be sprayed with 70% ethanol for decontamination.
Glass, sharp, plastic and cell culture waste must be disposed according to the Biomedicum Helsinki waste handling rules and procedures, see below.
Gloves, lab coat and, if appropriate, mask/safety goggles must be worn at all times.
A record has to be kept on all experiments. For each experiment, record date, organisms used, and if the material is to be transferred for storage – location of the storages.
Cell culture plates or trays holding the plates must all contain the following information: Date, your initials and cell line, passage number, culture media type, type of treatment. These plates are to be kept in the CO2 incubator marked with the Biohazard-sign.
Waste disposal

text:
holding the plates must all contain the following information: Date, your initials and cell line, passage number, culture media type, type of treatment. These plates are to be kept in the CO2 incubator marked with the Biohazard-sign.
Waste disposal
The amount of BSL1-class waste is small, max 20kg/week solid waste and approx. 10L liquid waste/week.
Solid waste
Cell culture and patient material- derived waste is collected into the autoclavable waste bags outside the laminar. When full, this bags are closed and placed into another big autoclavable bag to make a double-bag. These bags are sealed, marked with a red tape and an RPU (TOY) QR-code and “Polttoon – To be burnt” message on the paper tape, and taken either directly by the user or by the technician in charge to the refregirated waste room on the T-floor (Jatekylmiö) to be transported for burning.
Sharp waste Waste containing knives and needles is to be collected as sharp waste into hard-wall plastic containers (better commercially available containers with sliding lid). Markings: Sharp waste, RPU (TOY) QR-code. The container should be marked with red tape/sticker and brought to the corresponding waste container in the central waste room on the T-floor, smaller refrigerated waste room - Jatekylmiö. 
Glass waste Glass Pasteur pipettes are to be collected into the hard-wall plastic container and - when container is full – disinfected overnight with 2% Virkon disinfection solution, then dried and (if soda lime glass) disposed to the normal glass waste container (NOT to high-melting temperature Duran/Pyrex glass) located in the central waste room on the T-floor.

source_file: BSL2_documents_Färkkilä_lab/BSL1 and 2/Biosafety Manual for cell culture rooms B324a1a2.docx
text:
full – disinfected overnight with 2% Virkon disinfection solution, then dried and (if soda lime glass) disposed to the normal glass waste container (NOT to high-melting temperature Duran/Pyrex glass) located in the central waste room on the T-floor. Borosilicate glass Pasteur pipettes are discarded after disinfection to a separate Duran/Pyrex thermoresistant glass waste container located in the same waste room. Clean glass waste (reagent bottles, etc.) is disposed to either normal glass waste container or Duran/Pyrex thermoresistant glass waste container (depending on the glass type) in the same waste room, without prior disinfection. 
i)- and ii)- wastes are brought to the corresponding waste containers in the central waste room on the T-floor by the researchers or lab technicians in charge, not by the cleaning personnel!
Solid waste which was not in contact with the biological material can be put to the normal mixed or energy waste bins. These bins will be emptied by the cleaning personnel.
Liquid waste You can aspirate the cell/tissue culture media into the suction bottle. When the suction bottle is full, disconnect it from the suction system and add Virkon disinfection solution to a final concentration of 2% and let the liquid stay overnight and then pour the liquid into the sink. 
Spills and Emergencies
Spills in the working area and the laminar have to be disinfected immediately with 2% Virkon solution and then wiped out immediately, the surface must be then wiped with 70% ethanol (provided in spray bottle). Accidents in rooms B324a1,a2 must be reported immediately to the people in charge of biosafety (group leader and lab manager).
Before leaving the cell culture room
Clean up your things from the laminar.

chunk_id: BSL2_documents_Färkkilä_lab/BSL1 and 2/Biosafety Manual for cell culture rooms B324a1a2.docx::chunk_0003
source_file: BSL2_documents_Färkkilä_lab/BSL1 and 2/Biosafety Manual for cell culture rooms B324a1a2.docx
text:
ed with 70% ethanol (provided in spray bottle). Accidents in rooms B324a1,a2 must be reported immediately to the people in charge of biosafety (group leader and lab manager).
Before leaving the cell culture room
Clean up your things from the laminar. Wipe the laminar and all the other surfaces used with 70% ethanol from the spray bottle. Rinse the suction hose with water and then with 70% ethanol.
If full, take the waste bags downstairs to the central waste room. Decontaminate Pasteur pipettes waste if container is full, change suction bottle, if full.
Discard your gloves. Wash your hands.
GOOD LUCK WITH YOUR EXPERIMENTS!

Occupational accident
The University of Helsinki has obtained a more comprehensive insurance for its personnel for the exceptional circumstances from 30th of March 2020.

More information: helsinki.fi
Employees’ occupational accident insurance
The university’s employees are insured for occupational accidents and occupational diseases with LähiTapiola.
Employees should have access to the notice at the workplace.
The insurance number is 351-0926284-K.
The insurance is based on the Workers’ Compensation Act (459/2015).
The insurance company compensates costs resulting from occupational accidents and occupational diseases.
The insurance is valid around the world.
Accident insurance for university students and pupils.
What constitutes an occupational accident?
An occupational accident refers to a sudden, unexpected bodily injury caused by an external factor against the will of the injured party. An accident refers to injury that occurs:
in connection with working or performing a work-related task for the employer
in normal, work-related activities at the workplace premises
in the vicinity of the workplace during lunch hour or a break
while commuting between home and workplace (however not applicable to employees working at home)
in work-related training or recreational activities if the event was organised by the employer and the employer approved the participation.
an accident that has occurred during voluntary sports activities (including UniSport) does not constitute an occupational accident, even if the employer has paid for the activity.
After experiencing an occupational accident

After experiencing an occupational accident 
Immediately contact the HR coordinator of your local services.
- The HR coordinator acts as the contact person for the accident and fills in the electronic insurance certificate in the LähiTapiolan web service.
- The insurance certificate form is also available in English.
The HR coordinator will send the insurance certificate to your mobile phone or provide a written copy when you seek treatment.
- In urgent cases, the insurance certificate can also be sent to the clinic afterwards.
You can seek treatment at any clinic. Medical centre Mehiläinen is the current main partner of the insurance company, and those receiving further treatment are directed to Mehiläinen if available in the patient’s area. LähiTapiola also continues its cooperation with Dextra.
The insurance company will contact you directly to request any additional reports on the accident.
Inform your supervisor without delay of any absence or sick leave caused by the occupational accident.
- Fill in an absence notification through the SAP HR portal and mark occupational accident as the code for the reason for the absence.
Send a medical certificate to the employment services, P.O. Box 53, FI-00014 University of Helsinki.
The university will pay your full salary for 90 calendar days of absence caused by an occupational accident or occupational disease.
- Should the absence due to an accident exceed 90 days, you have the right to receive per diem allowance from the insurance company for one year starting from the date of the accident.

When your claim has been processed, the insurance company will send you the decision.
The medical institution and physician invoice the insurance company directly, but you will need to pay for the pharmaceutical costs.
- Apply for compensation for your costs by sending the scanned receipts and documentation to lttkorvaus@lahitapiola.fi (unencrypted address) or to the encrypted address https://secure.lahitapiola.fi. Add your account number and the accident number stated in the decision. Learn more about reimbursable expenses.
Reporting a serious occupational accident 
The supervisor or contact person for accidents must immediately report any serious occupational accidents (excluding accidents taking place on commutes) to the university’s occupational safety and health manager (Pirjo Kekäläinen). The employee have to report the fatal accidents, a serious accident refers to an incident causing a permanent or serious injury to the occupational safety and health division of the Regional State Administrative Agency. Do not move or change anything at the scene of the accident until it has been inspected. In addition to The employer’s responsibility to report occupational accidents is based on the Employment Accidents Insurance Act and the Act on Occupational Safety and Health Enforcement and Cooperation on Occupational Safety and Health at Workplaces (46§).
Puncture accident 
First aid:

text:
bility to report occupational accidents is based on the Employment Accidents Insurance Act and the Act on Occupational Safety and Health Enforcement and Cooperation on Occupational Safety and Health at Workplaces (46§).
Puncture accident 
First aid:
Rinse the puncture, wound or spatter area with plenty of water (at least 5 min) and then with A12 t solution (denaturated 80% ethanol solution or at least 70% alcohol).
Rinse the eyes and mouth with only water or eyewash.
Let the wound bleed to remove dirt from the wound. Do not squeeze the wounded area.
Contact your occupational healthcare services (metropolitan area tel. +358 (0)10 414 0666) or, outside office hours, the nearest available healthcare unit with emergency services.
Report the accident.
PLEASE NOTE! Meilahti Campus: if the accident includes identifiable human blood exposure, go to the emergency clinic of Haartman hospital.

Odottamattomien tilanteiden varalle laadittu toimintasuunnitelma
Jos Biomedicum Helsinki rakennuksen eristystason 1 soluviljelyhuonetilat, B324a1 ja B324a2 (Liite 1: salassapidettävät tiedot), joudutaan äkillisesti poistamaan käytöstä esim. vesivahingon vuoksi, kaikki tiloissa oleva nestemäinen soluviljely joko tuhottaisiin 2% Virkon-liuoksella tai mahdollisuuksien mukaan siirrettäisiin pakastimeen (Käytävä/Laitetila B320) säilöön. Kiinteä jäte tuhottaisiin jätesuunnitelman mukaisesti, eli mahdollisuuksien mukaan vietäisiin P-kerroksen kylmäsäiliöön odottamaan polttokäsittelyyn viemistä, mikäli tämä tila ei olisi vesivahingon kohteena. Käyttömäärät ovat melko pieniä, joten suurimittainen leviäminen ympäristöön ei ole mahdollista. Lisäksi soluviljelmät eivät selviä soluviljelyolosuhteidene (lämpötila, pH, steriiliys).
BSL-1 soluviljelmät eivät sinällään aiheuta lisää riskiä tulipalotilanteissa, sillä ne tuhoutuvat melko helposti lämmön vaikutuksesta eivätkä leviä aerosolina. Jos esim. työskentelyssä käytetty HEPA-filtterillinen laminaarikaappi tai solujen kasvatukseen käytetty inkubaattori rikkoutuisi, työskentely keskeytettäisiin ja materiaali joko tuhottaisiin asianmukaisesti, siirrettäisiin muihin laboratoriotiloissa oleviin soluviljelykasvatuskaappeihin tai mahdollisuuksien mukaan siirrettäisiin pakastimeen säilöön.
vastuuhenkilö: Anastasiya Chernenko (044-0526706)
varavastuuhenkilö: Ashwini Nagaraj (040-3275249)
Jos soluviljelmää sisältävää liuosta joutuu silmiin, tai suun tai nenän limakalvoille, altistunutta aluetta tulee huuhtoa runsaalla juoksevalla vedellä vähintään 10 minuutin ajan (silmien huuhtelupisteet B513h2 ja B521h2 vieressä). Tämän jälkeen ota yhteyttä työterveyshuoltoon (Mehiläinen Töölö, puh. 010 414 00).

i nenän limakalvoille, altistunutta aluetta tulee huuhtoa runsaalla juoksevalla vedellä vähintään 10 minuutin ajan (silmien huuhtelupisteet B513h2 ja B521h2 vieressä). Tämän jälkeen ota yhteyttä työterveyshuoltoon (Mehiläinen Töölö, puh. 010 414 00). Työskennellessä tulee käyttää hanskoja. Jos soluvvilelyliuosta joutuu iholle, desinfioidaan kädet 70%:lla etanolilla, ja pestään runsaalla määrällä saippuaa ja vettä. Jos soluviljelmä hanskoista huolimatta pääsee rikkoutuneelle iholle (esim. työtakin läpi imeytymällä) tai ihon alle pistohaavan kautta, pese altistunut alue 70%:lla etanolilla, ja sen jälkeen runsaalla vedellä ja saippualla, taputtele kuivaksi ja ota yhteys työterveyshuoltoon (Mehiläinen Työterveys, puh. 010 414 0666). 
Jos liuosta joutuu sentrifuugiin tai pinnoille, käsittele kontaminoituneet pinnat 1% Virkon-liuksella ja 70%-etanolilla. Jos virusta joutuu vaatteisiin, puhdista vaatteet 70% etanolilla ja/tai hävitä ne muun kiinteän jätteen mukana.
Jos solujätteiden inaktivointiin käytetty VIRKON loppuu, niin tuotetta kysytään lainaksi naapurilaboratorioista. Tämän jälkeen tuotetta tilataan lisää CityLab-laboratoriotarvikeliikkeestä.
Pakastimen (-80°C, laitetilassa AsP19) hajotessa materiaali siirretään ensisijaisesti huoneen B304b vieressä sijaitsevaan varapakastimeen. Jos -80°C pakastin joutuu epäkuntoon, asiasta ilmoitetaan Anniina Färkkilälle (050-5967786) sekä Anastasiya Chernenkolle (044-0526706). Vastuuhenkilöiden puhelinnumerot löytyvät myös pakastimien ovista.
Pisto- ja viiltohaavat. Ehkäistään käyttämällä suojavarusteita (hanskat, työtakit) sekä asianmukaisia työskentelytapoja ja välineitä. Ensiapukaappi, jossa mm. sidetarpeet pienten haavojen hoitoon, löytyy käyttävällä, huoneen B317a vierestä.

Ensiapukaappi, jossa mm. sidetarpeet pienten haavojen hoitoon, löytyy käyttävällä, huoneen B317a vierestä. Työterveyshuollon yhteystiedot: Mehiläinen Työterveys, puh. 010 414 0666.

text:
Biosafety Manual for cell culture room A227a1 (BSL 1)
Please read this manual carefully before starting any experiments. These rules apply to biosafety level 1 biological agents in room A227a1. However, no set of rules can substitute for careful working procedures, use of common sense and consideration of others.
General rules
Biosafety level 1 (BSL1) organisms used in this room are cell lines and cultured patient tissues. Their use will be contained in the laminars and incubators marked with Biohazard-sign. You will not be allowed to work in this room unless you have received adequate training in safe cell culture techniques.
The cell culture door is kept closed at all times. Sticky mat is put on the floor at the room entrance to reduce the amount of contaminants introduced into the room by researchers. 
No eating or drinking is allowed in the laboratory. (Not even chewing gum!)
Before and after the work all the surfaces in use and researcher’s gloves must be sprayed with 70% ethanol for decontamination.
Glass, sharp, plastic and cell culture waste must be disposed according to the Biomedicum Helsinki waste handling rules and procedures, see below.
Gloves, lab coat and if appropriate, mask/safety glasses must be worn at all times.
A record has to be kept on all experiments. This is your own responsibility and required by GTLK (Board of Gene Technology). For each experiment, record date, organisms used, and if the material is to be transferred for storage – location of the storages.
Cell culture plates or trays holding the plates must all contain the following information: Date, your initials and cell line, passage number, culture media type, type of treatment. These plates are to be kept in the CO2 incubator marked with the Biohazard-sign.
Waste disposal

holding the plates must all contain the following information: Date, your initials and cell line, passage number, culture media type, type of treatment. These plates are to be kept in the CO2 incubator marked with the Biohazard-sign.
Waste disposal
The amount of BSL1-class waste is small, max 20kg/week solid waste and approx. 5L liquid waste/week.
Solid waste
Cell culture and patient material- derived waste is collected into the autoclavable waste bags outside the laminar. When full, this bags are closed and placed into another big autoclavable bag to make a double-bag. These bags are sealed, marked with a barcode and “Polttoon – To be burnt” marking, and taken directly by the user to the central waste room on the P-floor to be autoclaved.
Sharp waste Waste containing knives and needles is to be collected as sharp waste into hard-wall plastic containers. Markings: Sharp waste, barcode or billing details. The container should be marked with red tape/sticker and brought to the corresponding waste container in the central waste room on the P-floor . 
Glass waste Pasteur pipettes are to be collected into the plastic container and when container is full – disinfected overnight with 2% P3-Triquart solution, then dried and disposed to the laboratory glass waste containers located in the central waste room on the P-floor. Clean glass waste (reagent bottles, etc.) is disposed to the normal glass waste containers located in the central waste room on the P-floor. 
i)- and ii)- wastes are brought to the corresponding waste containers in the central waste room on the P-floor by the researchers, not by the cleaning personnel!
Solid waste which was not in contact with the biological material can be put to the normal mixed or energy waste bins.

text:
t to the corresponding waste containers in the central waste room on the P-floor by the researchers, not by the cleaning personnel!
Solid waste which was not in contact with the biological material can be put to the normal mixed or energy waste bins. These bins will be emptied by the cleaning personnel.
Liquid waste When working with clean cells you can aspirate the media into the suction bottle supplied with final concentration of 2% of P3-Triquart disinfection solution. When the suction bottle is full, let the liquid stay overnight and then pour the liquid into the sink. Add new P3-triquart into the bottle before putting the suction system back together.
Spills and Emergencies
Spills in the working area and the laminar have to be decontaminated wiped out immediately with 70% ethanol (provided in spray bottle). Accidents in room A227a1 must be reported immediately to the people in charge of biosafety.
Before leaving the cell culture room
Clean up your things from the laminar. Wipe the laminar and all the other surfaces used with 70% ethanol from the spray bottle. Rinse the suction hose with water and 70% ethanol.
If full, take the waste bags downstairs to the central waste room. Decontaminate Pasteur pipettes waste if container is full, change suction bottle, if full.
Discard your gloves. Wash your hands.
GOOD LUCK WITH YOUR EXPERIMENTS!

How to behave in the cell culture room A521b1
Everybody wishing to use the cell culture facility has to read these instructions before starting their experiments.
These rules apply to the BSL2 (biosafety level 2) cell culture facility in room A521b1. BSL2 organisms used in this room are retroviruses and their use will be contained in the laminars and incubators marked with the Biohazard-sign. You are only allowed to work in this room if you have been properly instructed or otherwise have the skills required for safe microbiological/virological cell culture techniques.
The door to the cell culture room is kept closed all time. The personnel working in the laboratory will prevent unauthorized person from accessing the room. 
Eating or drinking is absolutely prohibited in the BSL2 cell culture room (not even chewing gum!). 
Use of needles and Pasteur glass pipets is not allowed when handling infective cell or virus material. (Attention: When cell are treated with detergent-containing buffer they are no longer infective.) When culturing clean cells, used needles and Pasteur pipets are collected into a plastic bottle. When the bottle is full, close it and discard as instructed in section 9 below.
As protective measures, everyone working in the room has to use double gloves, a laboratory coat to be used only in this room and safety glasses when deemed necessary (when using undiluted virus stocks). There is a rack for the laboratory coats next to the door. 
A record has to be kept on all experiments (date, organisms used, virus titers, RCV-test results etc.). This is the responsibility of each individual user.

hen using undiluted virus stocks). There is a rack for the laboratory coats next to the door. 
A record has to be kept on all experiments (date, organisms used, virus titers, RCV-test results etc.). This is the responsibility of each individual user. Also, official reporting on the use of genetically modified organisms is the responsibility of the principal investigator or other specified person within the user’s laboratory.
It is only allowed to produce retro- lentivirus stocks in this cell culture facility. 
Waste disposal:
The amount of BSL2-class waste is small, max. 10 kg/week solid waste and approx. 5 l liquid waste/week.
Liquid waste
Suction bottles must not be used with virus-containing infective liquid waste! Infective waste has to be collected into separate 2,5 litre bottle supplied with 50 ml Barrydin decontamination liquid. When the bottle is full let it be inactivated until the next day, after which you can safely discard it into the sink.
When working with clean cells you can suck the media into the suction bottles supplied with Barrydin (40 ml/ 2l bottle; dilution 5%). When the suction bottle is full pour the liquid into the sink and add new Barrydin into the bottle before putting the suction system back together. 
Solid waste
Waste produced when using undiluted virus stocks (e.g. when making infections with recombinant lenti- or retroviruses) is collected into a small waste bag inside the laminar. This bag is closed inside the laminar after use and placed inside a big autoclavable double-bag. These bags are sealed separately, marked with a bar code, and taken directly by the user to the central waste room on 0-floor to be autoclaved.

aminar. This bag is closed inside the laminar after use and placed inside a big autoclavable double-bag. These bags are sealed separately, marked with a bar code, and taken directly by the user to the central waste room on 0-floor to be autoclaved. 
Virusta sisältävien solujen käsittelyssä (infektion jälkeen tapahtuva työskentely, jolloin ei siis käsitellä laimentamatonta infektiivistä virusta) is collected into a small waste bag inside the laminar. This bag is closed inside the laminar after use and placed inside a big autoclavable double-bag. When full, these big bags are sealed separately, marked with a bar code and taken to the central waste room on 0-floor to be autoclaved. The big waste bag in use is protected with a lid marked with a Biohazard-sign.
Waste containing knives and needles as sharp waste. Markings: Sharp waste, bar code (of the waste producer) or billing details. The container should be marked with red tape/sticker.
Glass waste together with regular solid waste but packed so that it does not cause harm to cleaning personnel (in hard bottles or similar). 
Clean, non-infective (BSL1 class) solid waste is divided into mixed and energy waste as usual. Cleaning personnel will then empty these bins as usual.
Cell culture plates and bottles in the incubator marked with the Biohazard-sign must be marked with the user’s name/initials, date, cell line and the virus used (when applicable). It is advisable to use trays on the shelves.
Emergencies and decontamination of spills: 
Spills in the working area and the laminar have to be decontaminated with 2% Barrydin solution (provided in a spray bottle).
Accidents in room A521b1 must be reported immediately to the people in charge of biosafety: 
Kari Alitalo
Tapio Tainola
Pirjo Laakkonen
